tagline: Creating communities with clients who share our values.

slides.title: Raw Land Before Development Begins

narration.script: This aerial shows a heavily wooded parcel of land, essentially untouched. No cleared areas, no roads, no infrastructure. This is what a project site looks like before any engineering or planning work begins. In land development, this is called raw land, and every project in the firm's portfolio started here. Before any drawings are produced, the team studies what they're working with: existing vegetation, topography, surrounding roads, wetlands, and property boundaries. As an intern, when you're assigned to a new project, this is your mental starting point. Look at aerial imagery early, before the first plan is drawn. Understanding the original site conditions helps you follow design decisions, catch errors, and contribute more usefully to team conversations.

images.label: Wide aerial satellite view of a densely forested undeveloped parcel with surrounding roads and existing neighborhoods visible at the edges.

blocks.text: Before reading any plan sheet, pull up an aerial of the raw site so you understand what existed before the design began.

slides.title: Raw Land: Site Conditions Near Road Frontage

narration.script: This aerial shows raw land sitting directly adjacent to a highway. Notice the tree cover, the water feature in the upper left, and the road frontage along the bottom. These are three of the first things an engineer or planner looks at during the feasibility phase: where does the site connect to public roads, what natural features are present, and what constraints will shape the design? Access drives, utility corridors, and stormwater routing are all influenced by where the site meets the road network. As an intern, you'll encounter aerials like this at the start of almost every project. The water feature suggests a potential wetland delineation effort. The road frontage drives intersection and access design. The tree cover hints at clearing and grading limits. Nothing in this image is just background. It's all information the design team is actively reading.

images.label: Aerial satellite view of a forested undeveloped parcel with a highway visible at the bottom edge and a small pond or water feature in the upper left.

blocks.text: Road frontage, water features, and tree cover visible in early aerials directly shape design decisions made weeks or months later.

slides.title: Raw Parcel with Property Boundary Overlay

narration.script: This image shows a raw parcel of land with property boundary lines drawn over the aerial. Those white lines define the legal limits of the site, which is separate from what you can observe on the ground. In early project work, one of the first tasks is reconciling the legal boundary with the physical conditions. Trees, slopes, and water features don't respect property lines, and that tension is something every design has to resolve. As an intern, you may be asked to work with parcel data in CAD or to review boundary information against a site survey. Understanding that the drawn line and the physical world are two different things, and that your job involves bridging them, is a foundational mindset for land development work. Always know where the property line is before asking what can go there.

images.label: Aerial satellite image of a forested parcel with white property boundary lines overlaid, adjacent to a highway visible at the top of the frame.

blocks.text: Property boundary lines define what the team can legally design within, and reconciling those limits against actual site conditions is one of the first tasks on any project.

slides.title: Concept Plan Detail: Zoomed Site Layout

narration.script: What you're looking at is a concept plan overlay on aerial imagery, zoomed into one section of a larger development. The colored areas represent different proposed uses: residential lots, open space, community features, and the road network connecting them. This is one of the earliest formal deliverables a planning team produces, and it's intentionally approximate. A concept plan isn't drawn to construction standards. It exists to show a client, a county planner, or a reviewing agency what the team is proposing before detailed engineering begins. As an intern, you may help prepare exhibits like this in CAD or graphics software. Pay attention to how the design responds to existing conditions. Notice how roads connect to surrounding infrastructure and how open space clusters around natural constraints. Concept plans translate vision into something a room full of people can review and react to.

images.label: Close-up aerial image of a proposed development site with a colorful concept plan overlay showing residential lots, open space buffers, road networks, and community amenity areas.

blocks.text: A concept plan is a communication tool first and a design tool second. Its job is to show intent, not precision.

slides.title: Full Concept Plan: Master Development Layout

narration.script: This is the full concept plan for a large residential development, shown at a scale that reveals how the project fits into its surroundings. You can see the complete proposed road network, the placement of residential clusters, open space corridors, and how the development connects to existing neighborhoods and roads nearby. This is the big-picture view that drives every design decision that follows. Concept plans like this are the result of collaboration between planners, engineers, and clients before a single construction detail has been drawn. For an intern, this image is a reminder to always understand the whole site before diving into a specific plan sheet. When you're working on a grading plan or utility layout, the concept plan is the document that explains why streets go where they go and why open space sits where it sits. It's the map behind the map.

images.label: Wide aerial view of a large residential development concept plan overlaid on satellite imagery, showing the full proposed road network, residential clusters, open space corridors, and surrounding existing neighborhoods.

blocks.text: Always locate your current task within the master concept plan so you understand the intent behind every design element you're drafting or reviewing.

slides.title: Active Grading: Site Under Construction

narration.script: This aerial shows a site in active construction. The trees are gone, the soil is exposed and graded, and you can see the early formation of road beds and building pads. This is what the design team's drawings become in the field. Everything on the grading plan, the erosion control plan, and the construction documents is being executed out here in real time. For an intern, this stage is one of the most important to understand visually. When you review plans in the office, it's easy to treat them as abstract drawings. But this image is a reminder that those drawings become a real landscape with real equipment and real consequences for errors. If you ever have the chance to visit a construction site during your internship, take it. The field experience will make every plan sheet you work on afterward more meaningful.

images.label: Aerial view of a construction site with trees cleared, soil exposed and graded to a red-orange color, and early road and building pad infrastructure taking shape adjacent to a major highway.

blocks.text: Construction sites are where design decisions become physical reality, so developing a visual sense of what a graded site looks like helps you produce better drawings in the office.

slides.title: Completed Development: Site After Construction

narration.script: This is what the finished product looks like. The same land that started as raw forested property is now a functioning built environment with streets, buildings, parking, and structured open space. This aerial represents the end result of years of planning, engineering, permitting, and construction work. For an intern, this image matters because it closes the loop. Every plan sheet you work on is a step toward producing something that looks like this. When you're drawing a utility layout or reviewing a grading plan, the question to keep in mind is whether this design will produce a site that functions well and holds up over time. Studying finished aerials also helps you develop a trained eye. Street widths, lot configurations, stormwater pond locations: you can trace how the design team's choices shaped the final outcome. Your drawings are the instructions the field follows.

images.label: Aerial view of a completed residential or mixed-use development with finished roads, structured parking, building footprints, and open space visible, set against surrounding development and highways.

blocks.text: Completed site aerials connect your office work to real-world outcomes. Study finished developments to build an eye for how good design decisions translate into a functional, built place.

slides.title: What Happens Without Stormwater Management

narration.script: This infographic answers the first question every intern should be able to answer: why does stormwater management matter? Without inlets, pipes, and detention facilities to manage runoff, rainfall on impervious surfaces like roads, rooftops, and parking lots has nowhere controlled to go. Streets flood, foundations take on water, slopes erode, and pollutants from paved surfaces wash directly into streams and rivers. Every callout in this image represents a real failure mode that stormwater design is engineered to prevent. At Rodgers, stormwater management is a core component of every site civil project. It's required by local and state regulations and is one of the elements most scrutinized during plan review. When a project manager asks you to route drainage or size an inlet, this image is the reason that work matters.

images.label: Annotated infographic showing a flooded suburban street scene with callouts identifying flooded streets, impervious surface runoff, missing drainage infrastructure, water pooling around building foundations, soil erosion on slopes, and sediment and pollution reaching waterways.

blocks.text: Stormwater design isn't background work. It's a safety and environmental requirement, and every inlet, pipe, and pond you design prevents one or more of the failure modes shown in this image.

slides.title: Stormwater Flow Path: From Rainfall to Discharge

narration.script: This diagram traces the journey stormwater takes from the moment rain hits a site to the point it leaves the property. Rainfall lands on the roof and flows to downspouts. It also runs off parking lots and drive aisles as surface flow. Both paths direct water toward inlets and catch basins, the collection points that feed the underground storm drain network. Pipes convey that water to a detention or treatment facility where peak flow is controlled and water quality is improved before a controlled release at the outfall. This seven-step path is the mental model behind every stormwater plan Rodgers produces. When you're working on a drainage plan, keep this sequence in your head: where does the rain land, where does it flow on the surface, how does it get collected, where does it go underground, and where does it eventually leave the site.

images.label: 3D illustration of a commercial site with a numbered stormwater flow path showing rainfall, rooftop runoff, pavement runoff, collection at inlets, underground storm drain pipes, detention and treatment, and final discharge to a receiving water body.

blocks.text: Every stormwater design follows the same basic flow path. Keeping the sequence from rainfall to outfall in mind helps you trace and verify drainage logic on any plan sheet.

slides.title: Stormwater Drainage System: Components and Connections

narration.script: This illustration gives you a complete picture of the stormwater system from end to end. Roof drainage feeds downspouts that connect underground. Catch basins and inlets at the street collect roadway runoff and trap debris and sediment. Storm drain manholes provide access points for inspection and maintenance along the pipe runs. The underground pipes convey everything to a detention pond where stormwater is temporarily stored, sediment settles, and peak flow rates are reduced before the outfall structure releases treated water in a controlled way to the receiving waterway. Each component shown here has a standard detail, a plan symbol, and a specification that governs how it's designed and built. This image is a map of the system you'll be designing piece by piece on individual plan sheets. Knowing how all the pieces connect helps you design each one more intelligently.

images.label: Detailed 3D cutaway illustration of a stormwater drainage system showing rooftop drainage, downspout connection, catch basin and inlet, storm drain manholes, underground storm drain pipes, a detention pond, and an outfall structure with labeled callouts for each component.

blocks.text: Every component in a stormwater system serves a specific function in the collection-conveyance-treatment-discharge chain. Knowing how they connect helps you design each piece with the full system in mind.

slides.title: Gutter Flow and Curb Inlet in Action

narration.script: This photo shows the stormwater system doing exactly what it's designed to do: water flows along the gutter, directed by the curb and the 2% cross slope of the road, and drops into the curb inlet grate where it enters the underground storm drain network. The debris and leaves partially blocking the grate are a reminder that inlet capacity depends on maintenance as much as design. If grates clog, water backs up in the gutter and eventually onto the traveled way. At Rodgers, inlet sizing and spacing are calculated based on how much runoff a drainage area generates and how far water can travel in the gutter before it spills into traffic lanes. When you're placing inlets on a drainage plan, this is what you're designing for. The gutter carries water to the inlet, and both have to be sized correctly to keep the street clear.

images.label: Ground-level photo of water flowing along a wet street gutter and dropping into a cast iron curb inlet grate mounted in a concrete curb, with snow visible on the sidewalk and a street receding in the background.

blocks.text: Inlet placement and sizing must account for gutter capacity and debris accumulation. If either is undersized or blocked, the result is water in the road, which is both a design failure and a safety problem.

slides.title: Catch Basin Section Detail: Internal Components

narration.script: This cross-section detail shows what's inside a catch basin, one of the most common drainage structures on a site civil project. At the top, a cast iron grate and frame sit at finished grade, set in mortar. Adjusting rings below allow the top elevation to be fine-tuned during installation. The barrel is made of precast concrete sections stacked to the required height. Inlet and outlet pipes connect through the barrel walls, grouted to prevent infiltration. At the bottom, a sediment sump collects debris and solids that wash in with stormwater, keeping them out of the downstream pipe system. The concrete base sits on a compacted granular foundation. At Rodgers, standard catch basin details like this are referenced throughout plan sets and construction specifications. When you draw or label a catch basin on a plan sheet, this is the physical structure those symbols represent.

images.label: Technical cross-section diagram titled Catch Basin Section Detail showing a cast iron grate and frame, adjusting rings, precast concrete top slab, precast barrel sections, grouted inlet and outlet pipes, a sediment sump at the bottom, a concrete base slab, and a compacted granular foundation.

blocks.text: Understanding catch basin internals helps you draw them accurately, specify them correctly, and recognize when a detail shown on a plan may not match standard construction practice.

slides.title: Catch Basin Installation: Step by Step

narration.script: This infographic shows how a precast catch basin gets installed in the field. The sequence starts with excavating to the required depth and compacting a gravel bedding base. The base section is then set into position, with the connecting storm pipe already threaded through the knockout opening. Additional precast sections are stacked to bring the structure to the required rim elevation. The top slab and grate are placed last, and the excavation is backfilled and compacted around the structure. The main photo shows the base unit being lifted by crane with the storm pipe already connected and the gravel bed visible below. This matters because the pipe goes in before the basin is lowered, not after. Understanding installation sequences like this helps you produce details and specifications that are actually buildable and don't create conflicts for the construction crew in the field.

images.label: Construction photo infographic showing a precast catch basin being lifted by crane into an excavated gravel-bedded trench with a storm pipe already connected, with smaller panel photos showing the four installation steps: excavation and bedding, base section placement, section stacking, and top slab and grate installation.

blocks.text: Catch basin installation follows a precise sequence where the storm pipe must be connected before the structure is lowered. Understanding field sequencing helps you produce details and specifications that work in the real world.

slides.title: Inside a Storm Drain Manhole: Knockout and Access Steps

narration.script: This photo is taken looking straight down into a precast concrete storm drain manhole before it's been fully connected. The flat precast section at the bottom is a knockout, a pre-formed area in the barrel wall that can be chipped or cut out to accept a connecting pipe at the required elevation and direction. Iron step rungs on the interior wall allow maintenance crews to safely descend into the structure for inspection and cleaning. Storm drain manholes serve a critical function beyond connection: they're the access points that keep the underground pipe network maintainable over its full service life. At Rodgers, manhole locations are coordinated with pipe runs, slopes, and access requirements. When you're laying out a storm drain network, manhole spacing and accessibility are design constraints you need to account for, not details you add at the end.

images.label: Looking-down photo inside a cylindrical precast concrete storm drain manhole showing a flat knockout panel at the bottom center, and iron step rungs mounted on the interior barrel wall for maintenance access.

blocks.text: Storm drain manholes aren't just connection points. They're the access infrastructure that keeps the entire underground pipe network maintainable for decades. Place them deliberately on your drainage plans.

slides.title: Roadside Swale with Curb Inlet

narration.script: This image shows a vegetated roadside swale running parallel to a street, with a curb inlet at the curb line collecting flow from the road surface. Swales are open channel drainage features that collect and convey stormwater through shallow, vegetated depressions rather than underground pipes. They're often a lower-cost alternative to full curb and gutter, and they provide some water quality benefit by slowing flow and allowing sediment to settle before runoff reaches an inlet or outfall. This combination of swale and inlet is common on lower-density residential streets, rural roads, and commercial site perimeters where right of way is available. At Rodgers, the choice between curb-and-gutter drainage and open swale drainage depends on site conditions, jurisdiction requirements, and project type. As an intern, knowing both systems and when each is appropriate is practical design judgment you'll develop on your first drainage project.

images.label: Street-level view of a vegetated grass swale running along the edge of a roadway with a curb inlet visible at the curb line, adjacent to a paved road and parking area.

blocks.text: Open swales are a viable and often preferred alternative to curb and gutter in appropriate settings. Understanding when each system fits helps you make better drainage design decisions and ask better questions early in a project.

slides.title: Storm Drain Pipe Installation: Trench, Bedding, and Placement

narration.script: This photo shows storm drain pipe installation in progress. A large corrugated pipe sits in a trench on a prepared gravel bedding layer, with a worker guiding it into position and an excavator supporting the operation. The labeled steps walk through the full process: trench excavation, bedding material placement, pipe installation, backfill and compaction, and structure connections. Each step has specific requirements governed by the project specifications. Bedding material type and thickness control how the pipe is supported and how loads from above are distributed. Compaction of the backfill above the pipe is critical to prevent settlement that can damage the pipe or the road surface above it. At Rodgers, storm drain pipe design includes size, material, slope, cover depth, and bedding requirements. When you specify these on a plan set, the field crew installs to exactly those parameters, so getting them right matters.

images.label: Construction site photo showing a large corrugated storm drain pipe resting on a gravel bedding layer in an open trench with a worker guiding it and an excavator visible in the background, with an inset panel listing the five steps of stormwater pipe installation.

blocks.text: Storm drain pipe design isn't just about sizing. Bedding and backfill specifications directly determine long-term pipe performance. The parameters you put on a plan sheet become the exact instructions the field crew follows.

slides.title: Storm Drain Pipe Network in Civil 3D

narration.script: This screenshot shows a storm drain pipe network modeled in three dimensions in Civil 3D. Each structure is labeled with its rim elevation and invert elevations, and each pipe segment shows its size, slope, and length. The outfall at the end of the network is where the system discharges. Civil 3D's pipe network tools allow engineers to design and check the entire system in a model before anything is built, verifying that pipes have adequate slope, that inverts don't conflict with each other or with other utilities, and that the flow path routes correctly from inlet to outfall. At Rodgers, pipe network modeling in Civil 3D is standard practice on storm drain and sanitary sewer design. You may be asked to add structures, adjust pipe slopes, or generate plan and profile sheets directly from a network model. Understanding what you're looking at here makes all of that work considerably more intuitive.

images.label: Civil 3D screenshot showing a 3D storm drain pipe network in a looped configuration with labeled inlets, manholes, pipes, and an outfall, with annotation bubbles showing rim elevations, invert elevations, pipe sizes, slopes, and lengths for each element.

blocks.text: Civil 3D pipe network modeling lets you design and verify a drainage system in three dimensions before construction begins. Learning to read structure labels and pipe data in the model is a foundational CAD skill for any drainage design role.

slides.title: Storm Drain Plan: Commercial Site Layout

narration.script: This is a storm drain plan for a commercial development site, the type of drawing you'll be producing and reviewing regularly at Rodgers. The plan shows the proposed building surrounded by a network of storm drain pipes connecting curb inlets and manholes, all routing flow toward a detention pond. Each pipe segment is annotated with its size, material, and slope. Structure labels include rim elevations and invert elevations at both the inlet and outlet of each structure. The legend at right defines symbols for inlets, manholes, storm drain pipes, existing contours, and property lines. This is the type of plan sheet where you'll spend significant time as an intern. Before you draft or check one, trace the flow path from the highest point on the site to the outfall. Start at the high end and follow where the water goes. The logic of the design reveals itself that way.

images.label: Dark-background CAD storm drain plan for a commercial development showing a proposed building surrounded by storm drain pipes connecting curb inlets and manholes, routing flow to a detention pond, with pipe sizes, slopes, and invert elevations labeled and a legend at the right identifying plan symbols.

blocks.text: A storm drain plan tells a complete drainage story from inlet to outfall. Before drafting or reviewing one, trace the full flow path so you understand the drainage logic the design is built on.

slides.title: Rodgers Stormwater Drainage Plan: Riverbend Retail Development

narration.script: This is a real Rodgers Consulting stormwater drainage plan, sheet C5.1 for the Riverbend Retail Development in Lee's Summit, Missouri. It shows the complete storm drain network for the site: drainage area boundaries labeled A through D, storm drain pipes with sizes and invert elevations, catch basins and manholes with structure numbers, a proposed detention pond with a design water surface elevation, and flow direction arrows throughout. The drainage area table in the lower right documents the acreage, runoff coefficient, and impervious percentage for each basin. These values feed directly into the hydraulic calculations that determine pipe sizes and pond storage requirements. Every element on this sheet was designed, calculated, and coordinated with the grading, utility, and road plans before submission for agency review. As an intern, this is the professional standard your work will be held to. Study the sheet carefully and learn what every label represents.

images.label: Rodgers Consulting stormwater drainage plan sheet C5.1 for Riverbend Retail Development showing drainage area boundaries labeled A through D, storm drain pipe network with structure labels, a proposed detention pond with water surface elevation, flow direction arrows, and a drainage area table listing acreage, runoff coefficients, and impervious cover percentages.

blocks.text: A production drainage plan integrates drainage areas, pipe networks, structure data, and hydraulic calculations into a single coordinated sheet. Every label on this plan represents an engineering decision that was calculated and verified before it was submitted.

slides.title: Stormwater Detention Basin: How It Works

narration.script: This diagram explains how a stormwater detention basin works, one of the most important concepts in site civil engineering. When rain hits a developed site, it runs off faster than undeveloped land. A detention basin temporarily stores that excess runoff and releases it slowly through a controlled outlet, reducing the peak flow rate discharged downstream. The forebay at the inlet end captures sediment before water enters the main pond. The outlet structure uses a riser and orifice to control the release rate precisely. An emergency weir provides overflow protection during extreme storm events. The cross-section shows the 100-year water level above the normal water level, illustrating how the pond holds reserve storage capacity for major storms. At Rodgers, detention basin design is standard on nearly every commercial and residential development project. Understanding how the basin functions conceptually is the foundation for understanding why it's sized the way it is.

images.label: Educational diagram of a stormwater detention basin showing a plan view with forebay, main pond, outlet structure, emergency weir, and maintenance access path at the top, and a typical cross-section below showing normal water level, 100-year storm water level, inlet, forebay, pond storage, riser and orifice outlet, and controlled outflow to the receiving system, with a flow process summary at the bottom.

blocks.text: A detention basin controls peak runoff by storing it temporarily and releasing it slowly through a designed outlet. Understanding this function conceptually helps you interpret pond sizing and outlet design on any project.

slides.title: Detention Pond Construction: Four Stages

narration.script: This infographic documents the four construction stages of a stormwater detention pond. Initial excavation removes vegetation, topsoil, and bulk earthwork to rough grade. Grading and shaping fine-tunes the pond bottom, side slopes, and benches to the design dimensions and compacts the subgrade. Outlet structure installation sets the concrete riser, connects the outfall pipe and anti-seep collar, and places riprap for energy dissipation. Final stabilization establishes permanent vegetation on all slopes, removes temporary erosion controls, and prepares the pond for long-term operation. The erosion control note at the bottom is important: silt fence, stabilized entrance, inlet protection, and temporary seeding are all required throughout construction, not just at the start. At Rodgers, pond construction documents include grading plans, outlet structure details, and erosion control plans that must all coordinate. Knowing how the pond goes together helps you contribute to each of those documents more effectively.

images.label: Four-panel construction photo infographic showing detention pond construction stages: initial excavation with earthmoving equipment, grading and shaping of slopes and pond bottom, concrete outlet structure installation with riprap, and final stabilization with established vegetation and a completed outlet structure.

blocks.text: Detention pond construction follows a staged sequence from excavation through stabilization, with erosion controls required at every phase. Knowing the sequence helps you understand how grading, outlet, and erosion control documents all relate to each other.

slides.title: Completed Stormwater Management Facility

narration.script: This is what a completed stormwater management facility looks like after construction and stabilization. The basin is seeded and planted across the bottom and slopes, establishing the vegetation needed to resist erosion and support the facility's long-term function. Riprap protects the perimeter near the outlet where flow velocities are higher. The concrete outlet structure in the foreground is the control point for the entire facility, managing how and how fast water releases to the downstream system. This type of facility is the physical result of the detention design calculations, pond grading plans, outlet structure details, and erosion control measures that come together in a stormwater plan set. At Rodgers, SWM facilities like this one are a deliverable on nearly every development project. When you're reviewing facility grading or outlet design on a plan, this is what you're helping to build.

images.label: Panoramic photo of a completed stormwater management facility showing a seeded and planted basin with riprap-lined edges and a concrete outlet control structure visible in the lower foreground, with a commercial building and parking visible in the background.

blocks.text: A completed SWM facility is infrastructure that must function correctly for decades. The grading, outlet design, and vegetation plan all contribute to that long-term performance. Design all three with care.

slides.title: SWM Forebay Distribution Channel with Rock Check Structures

narration.script: This photo shows a distribution channel at a stormwater management facility, the inlet section that receives and slows runoff before it enters the main pond or forebay. The rock check structures along the channel act as energy dissipators, slowing the flow and encouraging sediment to drop out before water reaches the main storage area. The channel is vegetated to resist erosion at lower flow velocities. A commercial building is visible at the upper right, confirming this is part of an active development's stormwater system. At Rodgers, forebay and inlet channel design is part of every SWM facility plan. Channel dimensions, slopes, and stone sizing are calculated to handle design storm flows without scouring the channel bottom or sides. As an intern, details like this are easy to underestimate on a plan sheet, but a poorly designed forebay can compromise the entire pond's storage capacity over time.

images.label: Photo of a grassed stormwater distribution channel with rock check structures along its length directing flow down a gentle slope toward a stormwater facility, with a commercial building visible at the upper right.

blocks.text: Forebay channels and rock check structures are the first line of protection for a detention pond. Design them carefully or the pond will fill with sediment faster than expected and require costly maintenance.

slides.title: Riprap Outlet Protection at a Stormwater Discharge Point

narration.script: This photo shows riprap used as erosion protection at a stormwater pipe outfall. When water exits a pipe at velocity, it carries enough energy to scour and erode surrounding soil if nothing is there to absorb it. Riprap, large angular or rounded stone, is placed at outfalls and along channels to dissipate that energy and prevent erosion damage. The stone apron visible here surrounds the pipe opening and extends downstream to the point where flow velocity drops to a level the vegetated channel can handle without scouring. At Rodgers, riprap sizing and apron dimensions are calculated based on pipe size, slope, and design flow rate, then referenced on standard outlet protection details that are submitted with the plan set. When you see riprap called out at an outfall, it isn't a generic instruction. It's a calculated erosion control measure with specific stone sizing, layer thickness, and extent requirements.

images.label: Photo of a stormwater pipe outfall with a large riprap stone apron extending from the pipe opening down a graded slope, with grass visible on adjacent areas and a low headwall at the pipe opening.

blocks.text: Riprap at a stormwater outfall is a calculated design element, not a default detail. Understanding why it's sized the way it is helps you apply it correctly, especially on sites with high pipe velocities.

slides.title: Submerged Gravel Wetland: Advanced Stormwater Treatment

narration.script: This diagram introduces the Submerged Gravel Wetland, an advanced stormwater treatment system that goes beyond detention to actively improve water quality. Stormwater enters through a pretreatment forebay where larger sediment drops out. It then flows horizontally through submerged gravel media where filtration removes particles, microorganisms break down pollutants biologically, and wetland plants above the water surface absorb nitrogen and phosphorus. An underdrain at the bottom collects the treated water and conveys it to an outlet control structure that manages release rate and prevents overflow. This level of treatment is increasingly required by state and local regulations in sensitive watersheds. At Rodgers, understanding the full range of stormwater management options, from basic detention to advanced treatment systems, helps you design solutions that meet both quantity and water quality requirements. This is the more advanced end of stormwater design, but knowing it exists and how it works puts you well ahead for your first water quality project.

images.label: Educational diagram of a Submerged Gravel Wetland stormwater treatment system showing an inlet pipe, pretreatment forebay, fine and coarse gravel filtration layers, submerged water level, wetland vegetation above water, an underdrain collection system, an outlet control structure, and a five-step treatment process summary at the bottom.

blocks.text: Stormwater management isn't only about controlling flow rates. Advanced systems like submerged gravel wetlands are required on many projects to meet water quality standards. Knowing the full spectrum of solutions makes you a more capable designer.

slides.title: Typical Road Cross Section: Components and Dimensions

narration.script: This diagram introduces the typical road cross section, one of the most referenced documents in a land development project. It shows a 50-foot right of way with two 12-foot travel lanes, shoulders, grass buffers, and 6-foot sidewalks on each side. Below the pavement surface you can see the layered structure: asphalt on top, base course below, and compacted subgrade at the bottom. The 2% cross slopes on the travel lanes are intentional, directing runoff toward the curb and gutter where it's collected and routed into the storm drain system. At Rodgers, you'll encounter typical sections like this on nearly every road design project. The cross section drives all the other drawings: grading, utilities, and drainage all get coordinated relative to it. When you're asked to draft or review a cross section, start by understanding the right of way width and the back-of-curb dimension. Everything else is built outward from there.

images.label: Illustrated typical road cross section diagram showing a 50-foot right of way with two travel lanes, shoulders, grass buffers, sidewalks, and curb and gutter, with pavement layers including asphalt surface, base course, and compacted subgrade labeled below.

blocks.text: The typical road cross section is the master reference for road geometry. Every grading, drainage, and utility decision on a road project traces back to this document.

slides.title: Collector Road Typical Section: Technical CAD Detail

narration.script: This is a technical typical section for a collector road, the kind of drawing you'd find in an actual Rodgers plan set. It shows an 80-foot right of way, a 38-foot back-of-curb to back-of-curb road width, two 12-foot travel lanes, swales, and 10-foot sidewalks. The pavement structure table in the lower left breaks down each layer by thickness: 1.5 inches of asphalt surface course, 3.5 inches of binder course, 8 inches of aggregate base, and 6 inches of aggregate subbase over compacted subgrade. Cross slopes direct runoff toward the curbs at 2%. Unlike an infographic, this drawing is a construction document. It references standard details and notes that all subgrade must be compacted to specified standards. As an intern, learning to read a typical section like this is one of the first CAD literacy skills you'll build. It's the structural backbone of every road design.

images.label: Technical CAD-style typical roadway cross section for a collector road with an 80-foot right of way, showing lane widths, swales, sidewalks, and a pavement structure table listing asphalt surface, binder, aggregate base, subbase, and compacted subgrade thicknesses.

blocks.text: A technical typical section is a construction document, not just a reference diagram. Learn to read the layer thicknesses, dimensions, and notes because they directly govern how the road gets built.

slides.title: Roadway Horizontal Geometry: Curve Elements Explained

narration.script: This diagram explains horizontal curve geometry, which is the language behind how roads change direction in plan view. When a road needs to turn, it can't do so abruptly. It transitions through a circular curve defined by specific geometric elements. The PI, or Point of Intersection, is where the two tangent lines would meet if extended. The PC is where the curve begins and the PT is where it ends. The delta angle is the total change in direction through the curve. The radius determines how tight or gradual the curve is: a larger radius produces a gentler curve. The tangent length and long chord are used for layout and computation. At Rodgers, these elements appear in alignment tables on every road design plan sheet. You don't need to memorize every formula immediately, but knowing what each term means when you see it in a drawing or hear it in a meeting gets you into the conversation much faster.

images.label: Educational diagram titled Roadway Horizontal Geometry Plan View showing a circular curve with labeled elements including Point of Intersection, Point of Curvature, Point of Tangency, radius, delta angle, tangent length, long chord, and mid-ordinate, with a horizontal curve data table and reference notes.

blocks.text: Horizontal curve geometry defines how a road transitions between straight segments. Knowing the terminology, PI, PC, PT, radius, and delta, helps you read alignment tables and plan sheets with real confidence.

slides.title: Roadway Alignment Plan and Profile Sheet

narration.script: This is a plan and profile sheet, one of the most important drawing types in a roadway design plan set. The top half shows the horizontal alignment in plan view: the road's curve geometry, tangent lines, and curve data. The bottom half shows the vertical alignment in profile: existing ground elevation versus the proposed grade line, with crest and sag curves annotated at each grade change. The station table at the bottom ties both views together, listing elevations and grades at regular intervals along the road. At Rodgers, plan and profile sheets are core deliverables on nearly every road project. When you work with these sheets, build the habit of reading the plan view and profile view together, not one at a time. The plan tells you where the road goes horizontally. The profile tells you how it rises and falls vertically. You need both to understand what the road actually does.

images.label: Roadway alignment plan and profile sheet showing horizontal alignment with curve geometry in the top plan view and vertical alignment with existing ground and proposed grade line in the bottom profile view, with a station and elevation data table at the bottom.

blocks.text: A plan and profile sheet is the primary design document for road alignment. Practice reading both views together so you can build a complete three-dimensional picture of the proposed road.

slides.title: Vertical Alignment Profile: Grades and Curve Elements Explained

narration.script: This diagram breaks down the vertical alignment profile, which shows how a road's elevation changes along its length. The dashed line is the existing ground and the solid red line is the proposed grade. Where the grade transitions from uphill to downhill you have a crest vertical curve, the hill shape. Where it transitions from downhill to uphill you have a sag vertical curve, the valley shape. Each curve is defined by three key points: the PVC where the curve begins, the PVI where the grade lines would intersect if extended, and the PVT where the curve ends. Grade percentages are labeled along each tangent, and the positive or negative sign tells you whether the road is climbing or descending. Vertical curves affect sight distance, drainage, and ride comfort. When you encounter a profile view on a plan sheet, this diagram gives you the vocabulary to understand exactly what you're looking at.

images.label: Educational diagram titled Vertical Alignment Profile Explanation showing existing ground and proposed grade line with labeled crest and sag vertical curves, PVC, PVI, and PVT points, grade percentages along each tangent, and data tables listing key station and elevation values.

blocks.text: Understanding crest and sag curves, grade percentages, and the PVC-PVI-PVT sequence gives you the language to read profile sheets and participate in vertical alignment discussions on any road project.

slides.title: Curb and Gutter Installation: Step by Step

narration.script: This infographic walks through curb and gutter installation, one of the most common construction elements on a land development project. The sequence moves from subgrade excavation and preparation, to form setting using stakes and a string line for alignment and grade control, to concrete placement, surface finishing, and expansion joint tooling at regular intervals. Two details in the main photo are worth noting specifically. The forms and string line control the horizontal alignment and vertical grade of the finished curb, set directly from the design drawings. The 1 to 2 percent gutter slope toward the flow line ensures water drains properly rather than ponding in the gutter. At Rodgers, curb and gutter details appear on nearly every site and road plan. Understanding how the finished product is built helps you produce better drawings, because the dimensions and slopes you specify are exactly what the contractor will set their forms to.

images.label: Construction photo infographic showing curb and gutter installation with labeled steps for subgrade preparation, form setting, concrete pouring, surface finishing, and joint tooling, and callouts identifying forms and string line alignment and a 1 to 2 percent gutter drainage slope toward the flow line.

blocks.text: Curb and gutter forms are set directly from your design drawings. Understanding the installation sequence helps you appreciate why gutter slope and alignment dimensions are precise requirements, not suggestions.

slides.title: The Asphalt Paving Process: Base, Placement, and Compaction

narration.script: This infographic shows the three steps of asphalt paving as they happen on an active construction site. Base preparation comes first: the subgrade is graded and compacted and a stabilized base layer is installed to the required thickness. Asphalt placement follows, with hot mix delivered by dump truck and fed into a paving machine that lays it evenly at a controlled depth. Compaction is the final step, where steel wheel and pneumatic rollers press the asphalt to achieve proper density and a smooth, durable surface. Compaction is especially critical: asphalt that isn't rolled to specification will fail prematurely regardless of how well everything else was done. At Rodgers, the pavement structure in your typical section drawings corresponds directly to what you see being built here. Knowing the construction sequence helps you understand why layer thicknesses and compaction requirements in the specifications are non-negotiable.

images.label: Three-panel construction photo infographic showing asphalt paving steps: a grader compacting subgrade during base preparation, a dump truck feeding a paving machine during asphalt placement, and steel drum rollers finishing the surface during compaction.

blocks.text: Pavement is a layered system where each step depends on the quality of the one before it. Understanding the paving sequence helps you see why specification compliance in the field directly determines how long a road will last.

slides.title: ADA-Compliant Crosswalk and Curb Ramp

narration.script: This photo shows a completed ADA-compliant crosswalk and curb ramp at a commercial site. The striped crosswalk markings define the pedestrian crossing path across the drive aisle. The concrete curb ramp provides a graded transition from the parking surface to the sidewalk level, meeting ADA requirements for slope, landing width, and ramp dimensions. The sidewalk continues into the site, connecting the parking area to whatever destination lies beyond. Storm drain infrastructure is visible on the left and a fire hydrant sits to the right, two elements that appear routinely near curb lines and must be accounted for in your design. At Rodgers, ADA requirements are part of every project that includes pedestrian infrastructure. Curb ramp design, crosswalk placement, landing dimensions, and maximum slope percentages are all governed by federal accessibility standards. As an intern, every pedestrian connection you draft needs to be checked against those requirements before it leaves your desk.

images.label: Ground-level photo of a completed ADA-compliant crosswalk at a commercial site showing white striped crosswalk markings on asphalt, a concrete curb ramp landing, and a concrete sidewalk path leading into landscaped grounds, with a storm drain on the left and a fire hydrant on the right.

blocks.text: ADA compliance is a legal requirement, not a design preference. Every curb ramp and crosswalk in your drawings must meet specific dimensional and slope standards, so learn them early and apply them consistently.

slides.title: Intersection Construction: Annotated Aerial Overview

narration.script: This annotated aerial is one of the most information-dense images in this library, and it rewards careful study. It shows a full road intersection mid-construction with callouts identifying every major element: curb and gutter defining the pavement edge, storm drain inlets collecting runoff, median and traffic islands managing turning movements, utility structures for storm, sewer, and water being installed underground, temporary traffic control keeping the work zone safe, and a temporary stormwater pond managing construction runoff at the corner. Grading and earthwork are visible around the perimeter where subgrade preparation is still underway. The legend at lower right distinguishes new asphalt, concrete, graded earth, and stormwater flow direction. At Rodgers, an intersection design requires coordination across grading, drainage, utilities, pavement, and traffic engineering at the same time. This image shows how all of those disciplines land in the same physical space. When you're working on one piece of an intersection, this is the full picture surrounding your task.

images.label: Annotated aerial photograph of a four-way road intersection under construction with labeled callouts for stormwater pond, curb and gutter, storm drain inlets, intersection geometry, construction equipment, median and traffic islands, utility structures, grading and earthwork areas, and temporary traffic control, with a legend distinguishing new asphalt, concrete, graded earth, and stormwater flow direction.

blocks.text: An intersection brings every design discipline together in one place. Understanding how grading, drainage, utilities, pavement, and traffic control coexist on a single site makes you a more effective collaborator across the whole project team.

slides.title: What Is a Survey Control Network?

narration.script: This diagram introduces one of the most fundamental concepts in survey work: the control network. A survey control network is a set of fixed reference points with known, verified coordinates. Before any field measurements can be trusted, the crew establishes this network as the foundation. Everything else, topographic data, utility locations, property corners, gets measured relative to those fixed points. At Rodgers, the survey data that feeds into your CAD drawings came from a control network that was established and verified before a single elevation was recorded. As an intern, you won't be setting control points, but you should understand why they matter. When you spot a discrepancy in survey data, one of the first questions to ask is whether measurements traced back correctly to a verified control point. Accuracy starts at the foundation.

images.label: Infographic showing a surveyor operating a total station with labeled arrows identifying a fixed reference control point, a connected network of known coordinates, and the total station instrument.

blocks.text: Survey control networks are the invisible backbone behind every coordinate in a CAD file. Understanding them helps you evaluate whether the data you're working with can be trusted.

slides.title: Survey Crew Using a Total Station in the Field

narration.script: This photo shows a two-person survey crew at work. The crew member in the foreground is operating a total station, while the second crew member holds a prism rod downrange as the target. The total station measures horizontal and vertical angles together with distances, combining them into precise three-dimensional coordinates for every shot. This is how topographic data, utility locations, property boundaries, and construction stakes all get their coordinates in the first place. At Rodgers, survey data collected in the field like this is imported into Civil 3D and used to build the existing conditions model that your designs are based on. As an intern, when you open a survey file in CAD, this is the field process that created it. The quality of every plan sheet you produce is directly tied to the quality of this work.

images.label: Two workers in high-visibility vests on a job site, with one operating a total station on a tripod in the foreground and a second crew member visible downrange as the rod person.

blocks.text: Every coordinate in your CAD file came from a field crew like this one. Knowing that helps you understand why survey data is treated as a controlled input, not just background information.

slides.title: Construction Staking: Batter Boards and String Lines

narration.script: What you're looking at here is construction staking. The wooden batter boards and string lines stretched between them mark the exact location and elevation of a structure before concrete is poured or foundation work begins. This is how coordinates and elevations from the design drawings get physically transferred to the ground. The strings define corners, edges, and reference lines that the construction crew uses as a guide. The stakes are set by a survey crew working directly from the approved design drawings. This is one of the clearest examples of where engineering plans and physical construction meet. As an intern, understanding staking helps you see why correct dimensions, coordinates, and elevations on your plans matter so much. An error in your CAD model can become a misplaced wall or a wrong finished grade in the field.

images.label: Ground-level photo of wooden batter boards with orange string lines stretched between them on a grassy construction site, with a wooded treeline in the background.

blocks.text: Construction staking is the physical translation of your drawings. Every dimension and elevation on a plan has a real-world consequence you can eventually measure with a string line and a level.

slides.title: Utility Locating Marks on Pavement

narration.script: This is what a utility-marked surface looks like before excavation begins. Each color on the pavement represents a different type of underground utility, and the lines, arrows, and labels show the approximate location, width, and direction of each system. The manhole cover at the center of the image is a surface indicator of underground infrastructure, and you can see utility lines radiating outward from it in several directions. Before any digging happens on a civil project, utility locating is required by law, and what you see here is the result of that process. As an intern, you'll encounter utility data in your design work when checking for conflicts between proposed improvements and existing underground lines. This image is a reminder that the neatly drawn utility lines on your plan sheets represent real infrastructure that has been located, measured, and marked by people in the field.

images.label: Close-up of asphalt pavement covered in multicolored spray paint utility locating marks, with a manhole cover visible at the center and lines in green, orange, red, and pink indicating different types of underground utilities.

blocks.text: Utility locating marks represent real underground infrastructure your design must avoid. Treat utility data in your CAD files with the same seriousness you'd give a physical mark spray-painted on the street.

slides.title: Underground Utility Color Code Reference

narration.script: This is the utility color code reference every civil engineer should know. When you see spray paint marks on pavement, these colors tell you exactly what type of underground utility runs below. Red marks electric power lines and conduit. Yellow marks gas, oil, and petroleum lines. Orange marks communication and signal conduit. Blue marks potable water. Green marks sewer and drain lines. Purple marks reclaimed water and irrigation. Pink indicates a temporary survey marking. White marks a proposed excavation area. This standard is used industry-wide and is part of the APWA Uniform Color Code. At Rodgers, when you're designing near existing utilities or reviewing conflict reports, this system is what's being referenced by the field crews, the utility companies, and the inspectors. Memorizing these colors is one of the small, practical things you can do early in your internship that will make you immediately more useful in both field and office settings.

images.label: Color code reference chart showing eight colored circles labeled with the utility type each represents: Red for electric, Yellow for gas and petroleum, Orange for communication, Blue for potable water, Green for sewer and drain, Purple for reclaimed water, Pink for temporary survey marking, and White for proposed excavation.

blocks.text: Memorize the utility marking color code. It comes up constantly in field visits, utility conflict checks, and coordination meetings, and knowing it cold signals that you're paying attention to the right things.

slides.title: How to Read Contour Lines on Topographic Maps

narration.script: This diagram teaches one of the most essential skills for any civil engineer: reading contour lines. Each pair here shows a contour map pattern on the left and the terrain it represents in three-dimensional profile on the right. Closely spaced contours mean steep terrain. Widely spaced contours mean a gentle slope. Concentric rings indicate a hill or depression. Where two ring groups share a narrow connection between them, you're looking at a ridge or saddle between two high points. At Rodgers, you'll be reading contours on existing conditions plans, grading plans, and topographic surveys every day. Before you can design drainage, grading, or road alignments, you need to look at a set of lines on paper and mentally picture the shape of the land beneath them. Practice this skill early and deliberately. It becomes second nature faster than you'd expect.

images.label: Black and white educational diagram titled "How to read contour lines on topographic maps," showing six pairs of plan-view contour patterns on the left matched with their corresponding three-dimensional terrain silhouettes on the right, illustrating hills, peaks, ridges, slopes, and valleys.

blocks.text: Reading contour lines is one of the core visual literacy skills in civil engineering. The faster you can picture terrain from contours, the more useful you'll be to the design team from day one.

slides.title: Contour Spacing and What It Tells You About Slope

narration.script: This diagram teaches one of the most practical contour-reading skills you'll use every day: judging slope steepness from contour spacing. On the left, the contours are tightly packed together, and the side profile shows a steep, sharply terraced hill rising from 1440 to 1840 feet over a short horizontal distance. On the right, the same elevation range is covered by contours that are spread much farther apart, producing a broad, gradual slope in profile. The rule is simple: the closer the contours, the steeper the terrain. The farther apart they are, the gentler the grade. At Rodgers, you'll apply this reading constantly when evaluating grading plans, checking drainage patterns, and assessing whether proposed slopes are constructible and safe. Learning to estimate slope from contour spacing at a glance is a skill that will serve you on every project you work on.

images.label: Diagram comparing a steep slope and a gentle slope side by side, each showing the plan-view contour pattern at top and a labeled side-profile elevation view at bottom, with contour elevations ranging from 1440 to 1840 feet.

blocks.text: Contour spacing tells you slope steepness at a glance. Tight contours mean steep terrain, wide contours mean gentle grade. This is one of the most frequently used visual skills in civil engineering plan review.

slides.title: Topographic Survey Contours in CAD

narration.script: This image shows topographic contours as they appear in a processed CAD survey drawing. The orange lines are contour lines, each representing a constant elevation across the terrain. The labeled values at the bolder index contours, 15, 20, 25, and so on, mark key elevation intervals. The small X marks with decimal values scattered across the drawing are individual survey spot shots recorded in the field. The dashed line on the left appears to be a property boundary or unpaved road edge. This is how raw field survey data looks after it's been imported, processed, and drafted into a usable plan. As an intern, when you're working on a grading or utility plan, your design will sit on top of data like this. Understanding what you're reading, and being able to trust it, is essential to producing accurate work.

images.label: Light-background CAD plan showing orange topographic contour lines with elevation labels at intervals and small X marks indicating individual survey spot shot elevations, with a dashed boundary line on the left labeled "unpaved."

blocks.text: Topographic contours in CAD are processed survey data, not estimates. Learn to read the elevation labels and spot shots so you can verify that your design's relationship to existing grade makes sense.

slides.title: Existing Conditions Plan: Contours Only

narration.script: This is what an existing conditions plan looks like in Civil 3D when you're viewing topographic contours only. The gray lines are intermediate contours and the slightly bolder ones are index contours marking major elevation intervals. The pink linework at the top likely represents road geometry or boundary lines. In a Rodgers project workflow, the existing conditions plan is one of the first CAD deliverables produced after survey data is processed. Every design that follows, grading, drainage, utilities, roadway alignment, is built relative to this surface. As an intern, you'll spend a significant amount of time working within existing conditions files. Knowing what you're looking at when you open one means you can orient yourself quickly, identify high and low points, and understand what the terrain is doing before a single design element is placed.

images.label: Dark-background Civil 3D CAD plan showing topographic contour lines in gray and white across a complex terrain, with pink linework at the top of the image likely indicating road or boundary geometry.

blocks.text: An existing conditions plan is the ground truth your design is built on. Learn to read it fluently before trying to interpret any design layer placed on top of it.

slides.title: Existing Conditions Plan: Contours with Environmental Features

narration.script: This is the existing conditions plan with environmental and infrastructure features layered on top of the topographic base. You can see the wetland boundary marked in blue and labeled W, alongside what appear to be easement corridors, road centerlines, and utility alignments shown in different colors. This level of information is what the design team works with when evaluating what a site can and cannot accommodate. At Rodgers, understanding environmental constraints early is critical to good design. Wetlands, floodplains, and regulated buffers directly affect where roads can go, where utilities can be routed, and how much of a site can be graded or developed. As an intern, when you encounter a feature on an existing conditions plan that you don't recognize, look it up before assuming it's background noise. Every layer on this plan is there because it affects the design.

images.label: Dark-background Civil 3D CAD plan showing topographic contours overlaid with multicolored environmental and infrastructure features, including a blue-outlined wetland boundary labeled W, road corridor lines, easements, and utility alignments in red, orange, and yellow.

blocks.text: An existing conditions plan with environmental layers shows both what the land allows and what it restricts. Understanding those constraints early helps you ask better questions and avoid costly design errors.

slides.title: Survey Points Imported into Civil 3D

narration.script: This screenshot shows survey points imported directly into Civil 3D and displayed alongside a design in progress. The numbered points are individual field measurements, each tagged with a description code and an elevation. The red labels reading SSINV 8 PVC are sanitary sewer invert elevations at specific locations along a pipeline. The green and cyan points marked WHC and SSCO represent water house connections and sanitary sewer cleanouts. This is how survey data gets used at the design stage: not just as reference background, but as active data points tied to real-world locations and elevations that influence every design decision around them. As an intern, pay close attention to the description codes on survey points. They tell you what each point represents in the field. Learning to read point data accurately is one of the things that separates a capable CAD operator from an engineer who truly understands what they're building.

images.label: Dark-background Civil 3D screenshot showing numbered survey points with elevations and utility description codes, including sanitary sewer invert labels in red and water and sewer connection markers in green and cyan, overlaid on a road intersection design.

blocks.text: Survey points in CAD are not decoration. Each one is a real measured location. Learning to read their codes and elevations makes you a more reliable member of the design team from the start.

slides.title: Cut vs. Fill: The Core Concept in Earthwork Design

narration.script: This infographic covers the most fundamental concept in grading and earthwork: cut versus fill. Cut means removing soil where the existing ground is higher than your design elevation. Fill means adding soil where the existing ground is lower. Every grading plan is essentially a strategy for managing these two operations across a site. The goal in good earthwork design is to balance cut and fill as closely as possible. When you cut more than you fill, you have excess material that needs to be hauled off site, which costs money. When you fill more than you cut, you need to import material, which also costs money. Balancing the two minimizes truck trips, reduces cost, and lowers the project's environmental footprint. When you start working with grading plans in Civil 3D, cut and fill volumes are one of the first things the team will ask you to help track. Understanding what drives those numbers makes you much more useful in that conversation.

images.label: Colorful infographic split into two panels comparing cut and fill earthwork operations, with excavator and dump truck illustrations and a note at the bottom stating that balancing cut and fill reduces material transport cost and environmental impact.

blocks.text: Balancing cut and fill volumes is a core goal of grading design. Understanding this concept helps you read grading plans and contribute meaningfully to earthwork discussions from your first project.

slides.title: The Earthwork Construction Sequence

narration.script: This infographic walks through the sequence of earthwork operations from start to finish. It begins with the site survey and benchmark establishment, giving the crew the reference points they need to work from. From there, cut and fill areas are determined, grids and levels are marked in the field, and excavation and filling begin. After the bulk earthwork is done, levels are rechecked, compaction is verified, and final drainage slopes are established. This sequence matters because grading work doesn't happen all at once. It follows a deliberate order where each step depends on the accuracy of the one before it. For an intern, this overview helps you understand why things happen in a specific order on a construction site, and why a survey crew is involved both at the beginning and again near the end. Earthwork is iterative and precision-dependent at every stage.

images.label: Illustrated infographic showing eight steps of earthwork construction including site survey, benchmark setting, cut and fill determination, grid and level marking, excavation, level rechecking, compaction, and drainage slope establishment.

blocks.text: Earthwork follows a defined sequence where accuracy at each step sets up the success of the next. Knowing the order helps you understand why field survey checks happen throughout construction, not just at the start.

slides.title: Cut and Fill Road Cross-Section: Full Anatomy

narration.script: This cross-section diagram shows the full anatomy of a road built through cut and fill terrain, and it rewards careful study. On the left, the natural ground is higher than the road, so material is cut away and a back slope is created with a drainage ditch at its base. On the right, the natural ground is lower than the road, so fill material is placed to build up the embankment, tapering to a toe of slope at the bottom. In the center, you can see the road structure itself: roadbed, subgrade, base course, and surface course layered from bottom to top, with shoulders on each side of the traveled way. Right of way, clearing limits, and construction limits are all shown as nested boundaries around the full section. As an intern, diagrams like this one are references you will come back to often. Every label here corresponds to something you will encounter in plan sets, specifications, and field visits.

images.label: Technical cross-section diagram of a road showing a cut slope with drainage ditch on the left, a fill slope with toe of slope on the right, and the central road structure including roadbed, subgrade, base course, surface course, and shoulders, with right of way, clearing limits, construction limits, and property limits labeled as nested boundaries.

blocks.text: This cross-section shows how cut slope, fill slope, road layers, and right of way all relate to each other spatially. Memorizing this diagram early will help you read roadway cross-sections and plan sheets with significantly more confidence.

slides.title: Grading in Action: Before and After

narration.script: This before-and-after photo shows grading work at a scale that makes the outcome easy to see. On the left, the yard is freshly graded with exposed soil and drainage channels cut into the slope. On the right, the same space has been finished with timber retaining walls, terraced planting areas, stairs, and established grass. This is a residential example rather than a large-scale site civil project, but the principles are identical. Grading doesn't just reshape land. It creates functional, usable space. The retaining walls hold each terrace level, the drainage channels manage runoff, and the finished grade is what allows the space to serve its intended purpose. As an intern, this image is a reminder that the elevations and slopes you draw in CAD become surfaces that people will actually walk on, plant in, and drain water across. The grading plan is the design. This photo is the result.

images.label: Side-by-side photos of a residential backyard showing freshly graded bare soil with drainage channels cut into the slope on the left, and the completed terraced yard with timber retaining walls, stairs, planted grass, and landscaping on the right.

blocks.text: Grading transforms difficult terrain into functional space. The finished grades and drainage patterns you design in CAD become the physical environment someone will live, work, or park in. Keep that outcome in mind every time you place a contour.

slides.title: Understanding the Water Distribution System

narration.script: This infographic traces potable water from its source to the end user. Source water from rivers, lakes, or reservoirs is drawn into a treatment plant where it's screened, coagulated, settled, filtered, and disinfected before it's safe to drink. A pump station pressurizes the water and pushes it into the distribution system. A storage tank maintains steady pressure throughout the network. Water mains are the large pipes that carry pressurized water through the distribution system, and service lines branch off those mains to deliver water to individual buildings. At Rodgers, water main design is part of nearly every residential and commercial development project. When you're laying out a water system on a site plan, you're designing the segment between the existing public main and the connections to individual lots or buildings. Knowing the full system helps you understand what your design connects to and what it depends on.

images.label: Educational infographic titled Understanding the Water Distribution System showing the flow path from source water through a treatment plant, pump station, storage tank, water main, and service line to residential and commercial buildings, with a legend identifying water pipe, gate valve, and service connection symbols.

blocks.text: Water distribution design at Rodgers focuses on the local pipe network, but understanding the full system from source to tap helps you design the right pipe sizes, pressures, and connection points for your project.

slides.title: Understanding the Complete Sanitary Sewer System

narration.script: This infographic maps the entire sanitary sewer system from the building drain to the treatment plant. Wastewater from building plumbing flows through private laterals into public gravity sewer mains, which use slope to carry flow downhill toward trunk sewer lines. When gravity isn't sufficient because the terrain is too flat or the pipe has gotten too deep, a lift station pumps the sewage up into a force main that conveys it under pressure to a higher elevation. From there gravity takes over again until the wastewater reaches the treatment plant, where it's processed and the treated effluent is discharged to a receiving waterway. At Rodgers, sanitary sewer design involves gravity mains, manholes, laterals, and sometimes lift stations. Knowing how the full system works helps you understand where your design fits and what constraints, minimum slopes, cover depths, and connection points, you're designing around.

images.label: Large educational infographic titled Understanding the Complete Sanitary Sewer System showing numbered components including building plumbing, private lateral, public gravity sewer main, branch sewer line, trunk sewer main, lift station, force main, wastewater treatment plant, and discharge outfall, with a gravity flow direction indicator and key points legend.

blocks.text: Sanitary sewers rely on gravity flow for most of their length, making slope and depth the defining design constraints. Understanding the full system helps you design the local collection network with those constraints in mind from the start.

slides.title: Water and Sewer Development Process: Frederick County

narration.script: This flowchart maps the water and sewer development process for Frederick County and the City of Frederick, and it illustrates how involved utility permitting can be in a regulated jurisdiction. The process runs across three phases. Feasibility involves planning studies, annexation, comprehensive plan revisions, category changes, and zoning approvals before any serious design work begins. The design phase moves through concept planning, additional category changes for water and sewer service, offsite infrastructure coordination, permit applications, and final onsite improvement plan approval. The permit phase covers permitting documents, easement preparation, permit issuance, construction, and as-built submission. The timeframes across the top show that the complete process can span several years from initial feasibility to construction. At Rodgers, understanding the local approval process is part of serving clients well. As an intern, recognizing where a project sits in this sequence tells you a lot about what work is appropriate at that stage and what decisions haven't been made yet.

images.label: Swimlane flowchart titled Water and Sewer Development Process for Fred County and City of Fred showing three horizontal rows for feasibility, design, and permit phases with blue milestone boxes connected by arrows and approximate timeframes shown at the top ranging from two months to over twelve months per stage.

blocks.text: Water and sewer permitting in Frederick County is a multi-phase process that can span years. Knowing the sequence helps you understand why certain design decisions happen when they do and why early agency coordination is critical.

slides.title: Water and Sanitary Sewer Utility Plan: Residential Subdivision

narration.script: This is a water and sanitary sewer utility plan for a residential subdivision, the type of drawing you'll draft and review at Rodgers on virtually every residential project. The blue lines represent the 8-inch PVC water main running through the subdivision with gate valves and fire hydrants at regular intervals. The green lines represent the 8-inch PVC sanitary sewer with manholes and service cleanouts connecting to individual lots. Both systems share the street right of way and must maintain required separation distances from each other and from other utilities. The legend at upper right defines every plan symbol: water pipe, gate valve, fire hydrant, sanitary sewer, manhole, cleanout, flow direction, right of way, building, and property line. As an intern, before you draft or check a utility plan, you need to identify every symbol in the legend without hesitation. The plan tells the construction crew exactly what goes in the ground and where.

images.label: CAD utility plan sheet C3.1 for Pine Meadows Subdivision showing a residential street layout with blue 8-inch PVC water main including gate valves and fire hydrants, and green 8-inch PVC sanitary sewer with manholes and cleanouts at each lot, with a legend at upper right identifying all plan symbols.

blocks.text: A utility plan is one of the most construction-critical documents in a plan set. Learn the symbols, understand separation requirements, and trace both systems from connection to endpoint before you start drafting or reviewing one.

slides.title: Utility Trench Cross Section: Pipe Separation and Bedding

narration.script: This cross-section shows how multiple utility pipes share a single trench and the separation requirements that govern how they're positioned relative to each other. From the bottom up: the storm drain pipe sits at the lowest elevation on its own bedding layer, the sanitary sewer pipe is placed above it with a minimum 18-inch vertical clearance, and the water main sits at the highest elevation with another minimum 18-inch clearance above the sewer. Each pipe rests on its own granular bedding, shaped to support the pipe along its full length. Above the pipes, backfill is placed in stages: initial backfill around the pipe, select backfill above that, and compacted backfill to the subbase. Asphalt and aggregate subbase complete the section. At Rodgers, these separation requirements are checked during plan review. When you're designing utilities in a shared right of way corridor, this diagram defines the spatial constraints you're working within.
